annotator S.report_text:
POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI.

Its use of a PowerShell payload means that only legitimate system processes are utilized and that the malicious code execution can only be identified through enhanced logging or in memory.

WMI permanent event subscriptions can be used to trigger actions when specified conditions are met. Creating a WMI event subscription to run the backdoor.

We have observed APT29 use WMI to persist a backdoor and also store the PowerShell backdoor code.

APT29 wrote the encrypted and base64- encoded PowerShell backdoor code into that property.

APT29 then created a WMI event subscription in order to execute the backdoor.

The subscription was configured to run a PowerShell command that read, decrypted, and executed the backdoor code directly from the new WMI property.

This allowed them to install a persistent backdoor without leaving any artifacts on the system's hard drive, outside of the WMI repository. This "fileless" backdoor methodology made the identification of the backdoor much more difficult using standard host analysis techniques.

The WindowsParentalControlsMigration consumer was configured to silently execute a base64- encoded PowerShell command. Upon execution, this command extracted, decrypted, and executed the PowerShell backdoor payload stored in the HiveUploadTask text property of the RacTask class.

The POSHSPY backdoor is designed to download and execute additional PowerShell code and Windows binaries.

Downloading and executing PowerShell code as an EncodedCommand.

Writing executables to a randomly-selected directory under Program Files, and naming the EXE to match the chosen directory name, or, if that fails, writing the executable to a system-generated temporary file name, using the EXE extension.

Modifying the Standard Information timestamps (created, modified, accessed) of every downloaded executable to match a randomly selected file from the System32 directory that was created prior to 2013.

Encrypting communications using AES and RSA public key cryptography.

Deriving C2 URLs from a Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA).

Using a custom User Agent string or the system's User Agent string derived from urlmon.dll.

Uploading data in 2048-byte chunks.

Appending a file signature header to all encrypted data, prior to upload or download, by randomly selecting from the file types: ICO GIF JPG PNG MP3 BMP.

annotator L.report_text:
POSHSPY leverages two of the tools the group frequently uses
PowerShell The use of PowerShell to execute malicious commands or scripts. POSHSPY uses PowerShell as part of its toolkit.
Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) The use of WMI to maintain persistence on the system. POSHSPY leverages WMI as one of the main tools.
POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI The use of WMI to maintain persistence on the system. POSHSPY uses WMI to store and persist the backdoor code.
POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI The use of WMI to execute commands in order to avoid detection.
Its use of a PowerShell payload means that only legitimate system processes are utilized and that the malicious code execution can only be identified through enhanced logging or in memory The use of PowerShell payloads allows only legitimate system processes to be used, making detection of malicious code more difficult.
traffic obfuscation, Obfuscation of traffic and data.
created a new WMI class and added a text property to it in order to store a string value Creating a new WMI class and adding a text property.
APT29 wrote the encrypted and base64- encoded PowerShell backdoor code into that property Using PowerShell to run the backdoor
APT29 wrote the encrypted and base64- encoded PowerShell backdoor code into that property PowerShell code is encrypted and encoded in base64.
APT29 then created a WMI event subscription in order to execute the backdoor Creating a WMI event subscription to run the backdoor.
The subscription was configured to run a PowerShell command that read, decrypted, and executed the backdoor code directly from the new WMI property Running PowerShell commands to read, decrypt, and execute the backdoor code.
The subscription was configured to run a PowerShell command that read, decrypted, and executed the backdoor code directly from the new WMI property Decryption of the backdoor code.
install a persistent backdoor without leaving any artifacts on the system's hard drive Adversaries may attempt to hide artifacts associated with their behaviors to evade detection.
The POSHSPY backdoor is designed to download and execute additional PowerShell code and Windows binaries Download additional code to execute.
The POSHSPY backdoor is designed to download and execute additional PowerShell code and Windows binaries Run additional PowerShell code.
Downloading and executing PowerShell code Download additional code to execute.
Downloading and executing PowerShell code Run additional PowerShell code.
Writing executables to a randomly-selected directory under Program Files, and naming the EXE to match the chosen directory name, or, if that fails, writing the executable to a systemgenerated temporary file name, using the EXE extension This technique involves masking executable files with names and paths that seem legitimate or familiar to the user, such as using file names that correspond to random directory names under Program Files.
Modifying the Standard Information timestamps (created, modified, accessed) of every downloaded executable to match a randomly selected file from the System32 directory that was created prior to 2013 This TTP describes the action of modifying or manipulating file timestamps to hide malware activities, making it more difficult to detect and analyze operations performed on the system.
Encrypting communications using AES and RSA public key cryptography to the use of asymmetric encryption algorithms, such as RSA, and symmetric encryption algorithms, such as AES, to protect communications, making it more difficult for defenders to monitor and intercept communications.
Deriving C2 URLs from a Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) dynamically generate a list of command and control (C2) domains on the fly, making it more difficult for defenders to block or detect C2 domains because they are randomly or pseudo-randomly generated.
Using a custom User Agent string or the system's User Agent string derived from urlmon.dll POSHSPY uses custom or system-derived User Agent strings and employs custom or randomly generated cookie names and values
